1. Multi-Metabolite Signatures of exposure
Multi-Metabolite Signatures of exposure

Edit this page

Report an issue

Analytical workflow

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

This page presents a comprehensive and modular workflow for constructing a multimetabolite signature (MMS), covering all key steps from the raw data preparation to the downstream epidemiological applications. The framework is designed to be adaptable across studies.

I hope this offers a useful and inspiring starting point for developing your own MMS workflow! 🧪📊✨

Data import and preprocessing

Data structure

For a supervised MMS approach, the minimum required variables are:

  • subject identifiers,

  • metabolomics features,

  • at least one exposure variable.

Additional elements can be included depending on the study design, such as:

  • multiple exposures (for parallel signature construction),

  • covariates for adjustment or residualization,

  • metadata linking feature IDs to metabolite annotations.

Metabolomics platform

The metabolomics matrix should be clearly separated from participant-level metadata. In practice, this means defining which columns correspond to metabolite predictors and which columns correspond to exposures, covariates, and outcomes.

If you want a synthetic example for method development or teaching, a small simulation helper can be useful.

Example code
Metabolomics platform 
# Example: keep a vector with the metabolite feature columns
feature_columns <- colnames(analysis_df)[feature_start_col:ncol(analysis_df)]

# Convert to model matrix
feature_matrix <- as.matrix(analysis_df[, feature_columns])

# Optional: synthetic example data
# analysis_df <- simulate_example_data()

Exposure variables

The target exposure can be a single variable or several related variables if multiple signatures are built in parallel.

Example code
Exposure variables 
# Example target exposures for parallel MMS construction
target_outcomes <- c(
  exposure_a = "exposure_a_residual",
  exposure_b = "exposure_b_residual",
  exposure_c = "exposure_c_residual",
  exposure_d = "exposure_d_residual"
)

Outcome definitions

If the MMS will later be related to disease, mortality, or another endpoint, it is helpful to define those downstream datasets early so the training and testing workflow is explicit.

Example code
Outcome definitions 
evaluation_ids <- evaluation_registry_df %>%
  dplyr::pull(participant_id)

Before fitting MMS models, the data typically undergo residualization, cleaning, and standardization steps.

Typical metabolomics steps:

Missing value handling

Missing metabolite values should be addressed before model fitting, using a strategy aligned with the platform and study design.

A compact preprocessing helper can wrap missingness filtering, imputation, log transformation, and normalization in one step.

Example code
Missing value handling 
# Placeholder example
analysis_df <- analysis_df %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(feature_columns), ~ ifelse(is.na(.x), median(.x, na.rm = TRUE), .x)))

# Example reusable preprocessing helper
preprocess_metabolomics_data <- function(raw_data, id_col, target_col, feature_start_col,
                                         cutoff_columns = 0.2, cutoff_rows = 0.2,
                                         imputation = "median") {
  clean_data <- raw_data %>%
    tidyr::drop_na({{ target_col }}) %>%
    dplyr::distinct() %>%
    janitor::remove_empty("cols", cutoff = cutoff_columns) %>%
    janitor::remove_empty("rows", cutoff = cutoff_rows)

  variables <- clean_data[, seq_len(feature_start_col - 1)]
  metabolite_data <- clean_data[, feature_start_col:ncol(clean_data)]

  selected_data <- dplyr::bind_cols(
    dplyr::select(variables, {{ id_col }}, {{ target_col }}),
    metabolite_data
  )

  extra_covariates <- variables %>%
    dplyr::select(-{{ id_col }}, -{{ target_col }})

  preprocess_recipe <- recipes::recipe(~ ., data = selected_data) %>%
    recipes::update_role({{ id_col }}, new_role = "id") %>%
    recipes::update_role({{ target_col }}, new_role = "outcome")

  preprocess_recipe <- if (imputation == "knn") {
    preprocess_recipe %>% recipes::step_impute_knn(recipes::all_predictors())
  } else if (imputation == "lower") {
    preprocess_recipe %>% recipes::step_impute_lower(recipes::all_predictors())
  } else if (imputation == "mean") {
    preprocess_recipe %>% recipes::step_impute_mean(recipes::all_predictors())
  } else {
    preprocess_recipe %>% recipes::step_impute_median(recipes::all_predictors())
  }

  processed_data <- preprocess_recipe %>%
    recipes::step_log(recipes::all_predictors()) %>%
    recipes::step_normalize(recipes::all_predictors()) %>%
    recipes::prep(training = selected_data) %>%
    recipes::juice() %>%
    dplyr::rename(id = {{ id_col }}, target = {{ target_col }})

  dplyr::bind_cols(extra_covariates, processed_data)
}

Normalization

Normalization may be applied at the metabolite-feature level depending on the platform and preprocessing pipeline used upstream.

In the helper function above, normalization is performed through a recipes workflow after imputation and log transformation.

Example code
Normalization 
# Placeholder example for already selected feature columns
analysis_df <- analysis_df %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(feature_columns), ~ .x / sd(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))

Transformation (log scaling)

Log transformation is often used to reduce skewness and stabilize variance.

This is also built into the helper function above.

Example code
Transformation (log scaling) 
analysis_df <- analysis_df %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(feature_columns), log1p))

Batch correction

If samples were processed in multiple analytical batches, batch correction should occur before signature construction.

Example code
Batch correction 
# Placeholder: use the batch variable defined in your project
# analysis_df <- correct_batch_effects(analysis_df, batch_var = "batch_id", features = feature_columns)

Scaling (z-score)

Standardizing predictors is especially important for penalized regression models.

Again, the preprocessing helper already standardizes predictors after transformation, which is a useful template if you want a single entry point for data wrangling.

Example code
Scaling (z-score) 
analysis_df <- analysis_df %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(feature_columns), scale))

Study design & sample splitting

This section shows how to define training and testing populations in a reusable way.

Training vs test dataset

At minimum, an MMS workflow requires both a training dataset and a testing dataset. These can be obtained in two ways:

  • by using two separate datasets (one for model training and the other for external validation), or by defining subsets of individuals within a single dataset that are reserved for testing. In both cases, the datasets or subsets must contain the same variables and be similarly structured, or

  • by splitting a single dataset into training and testing subsets (e.g., random split).

Example code
User-defined training/test individuals 
  # Participants reserved for external or downstream evaluation
evaluation_ids <- external_registry_df %>%
  dplyr::pull(participant_id)

# Training population
train_data <- data %>%
  filter(!participant_id %in% evaluation_ids)

# Testing or external evaluation population
test_data <- data %>%
  filter(participant_id %in% evaluation_ids)
Random training/test splitting helper 
# Example helper-based split
split_dataset <- function(data, training_prop = 0.7, strata = NULL) {
  split_obj <- if (is.null(strata)) {
    rsample::initial_split(data, prop = training_prop)
  } else {
    rsample::initial_split(data, prop = training_prop, strata = {{ strata }})
  }

  list(
    split = split_obj,
    train_data = rsample::training(split_obj),
    test_data = rsample::testing(split_obj)
  )
}
strata

This note refers to the optional strata argument inside split_dataset().

Stratification helps keep the training and testing sets similar with respect to an important variable, usually the outcome or exposure grouping.This makes model training and evaluation more stable and more comparable. For example:

  • If your outcome is binary and rare, a purely random split might put too few cases in the test set.
  • If your target has strong class imbalance, stratification preserves roughly the same proportion in both subsets.

Use stratification when: - you have a categorical outcome like case/control - you have strong imbalance between groups - you want both train and test to represent the same population structure

You usually do not need it when: - the dataset is large and well balanced - the outcome is continuous and you are doing a simple random split

training_prop = 0.7

This note refers to the optional training_prop argument inside split_dataset().

This argument defines the proportion of individuals kept in the training dataset.

If you do not specify it, the default is 0.7, meaning that 70% of individuals are assigned to the training dataset and the remaining 30% to the testing dataset.

Cross-validation

Cross-validation is used to tune the regularization parameter and estimate the stability of the model.

If you want a more packaged training interface, a helper can wrap repeated cross-validation with caret::train().

Example code
Cross-validation 
set.seed(123)
cv_folds <- rsample::vfold_cv(signature_training_df, v = 10)

# Example helper for repeated cross-validation
cross_validated_model <- function(train_data, model = "glmnet", nfolds = 10,
                                  nrepeats = 5, tune_length = 10) {
  control <- caret::trainControl(
    method = "repeatedcv",
    number = nfolds,
    repeats = nrepeats,
    search = "random",
    verboseIter = TRUE
  )

  set.seed(42)
  caret::train(
    target ~ .,
    data = train_data,
    method = model,
    preProcess = c("center", "scale"),
    tuneLength = tune_length,
    trControl = control
  )
}

Internal validation

Internal validation can be performed by predicting the held-out fold or a pre-specified testing subset and then quantifying how well the MMS reproduces the target exposure.

Example code
Internal validation 
# Predictor matrix for training
training_feature_matrix <- as.matrix(signature_training_df[, feature_columns])

# Predictor matrix for testing
testing_feature_matrix <- as.matrix(signature_testing_df[, feature_columns])

Avoiding overfitting

Key strategies include:

  • separating tuning from final model fitting
  • evaluating performance in a held-out dataset
  • reporting coefficient stability across folds
  • using sensitivity analyses for downstream associations

Building the MMS

This section contains a generic template based on the original analysis: residualize the target exposure, tune a penalized model, fit the signature, and generate participant-level scores.

Feature selection

If the exposure of interest should first be adjusted for major confounders or correlated dietary components, residualization can be used before signature training.

For a single-metabolite screening step before multivariable signature building, a helper can fit metabolite-wise models, apply multiple testing correction, and return both the filtered results and residuals.

Example code
Feature selection 
# Example: residualize multiple target exposures against shared covariates
residual_model_specs <- list(
  exposure_a = exposure_a ~ total_energy + covariate_1 + covariate_2 + covariate_3,
  exposure_b = exposure_b ~ total_energy + covariate_1 + covariate_2 + covariate_3 + exposure_a,
  exposure_c = exposure_c ~ total_energy + covariate_1 + covariate_2 + covariate_3 + exposure_a + exposure_b,
  exposure_d = exposure_d ~ total_energy + covariate_1 + covariate_2 + covariate_3 + exposure_a + exposure_b
)

residual_models <- purrr::map(
  residual_model_specs,
  ~ lm(.x, data = analysis_df)
)

analysis_df <- analysis_df %>%
  mutate(
    exposure_a_residual = residuals(residual_models$exposure_a),
    exposure_b_residual = residuals(residual_models$exposure_b),
    exposure_c_residual = residuals(residual_models$exposure_c),
    exposure_d_residual = residuals(residual_models$exposure_d)
  )

# Example single-metabolite screening helper
single_metabolite_analysis <- function(data, train_data, exposure_feature,
                                       feature_start_col, covariates = NULL,
                                       threshold = 0.01, correction = NULL) {
  metabolite_columns <- colnames(data)[feature_start_col:ncol(data)]
  features <- c(exposure_feature, covariates)

  fit_single_model <- function(metabolite_name, predictors, dataset) {
    model_formula <- stats::reformulate(predictors, response = metabolite_name)
    stats::lm(model_formula, data = dataset) %>%
      broom::tidy() %>%
      dplyr::mutate(yvar = metabolite_name, .before = dplyr::everything())
  }

  extract_residuals <- function(metabolite_name, predictors, dataset) {
    model_formula <- stats::reformulate(predictors, response = metabolite_name)
    stats::lm(model_formula, data = dataset) %>%
      residuals() %>%
      tibble::tibble(value = .)
  }

  results <- metabolite_columns %>%
    furrr::future_map(~ fit_single_model(.x, features, dataset = train_data)) %>%
    purrr::list_rbind(names_to = "model_id")

  residuals_df <- metabolite_columns %>%
    furrr::future_map(~ extract_residuals(.x, features, dataset = data)) %>%
    purrr::list_cbind()

  colnames(residuals_df) <- metabolite_columns

  filtered_results <- if (length(correction)) {
    results %>%
      dplyr::mutate(p_value_corrected = stats::p.adjust(p.value, method = correction)) %>%
      dplyr::filter(stringr::str_detect(term, "target")) %>%
      dplyr::filter(p_value_corrected < threshold) %>%
      dplyr::arrange(p_value_corrected)
  } else {
    results %>%
      dplyr::filter(stringr::str_detect(term, "target")) %>%
      dplyr::filter(p.value < threshold) %>%
      dplyr::arrange(p.value)
  }

  list(
    results = filtered_results,
    residuals = residuals_df %>% dplyr::select(filtered_results$yvar)
  )
}

Model training

The original project used ridge regression (alpha = 0) to derive signatures for multiple related exposures. Below is a generic template for tuning lambda, fitting the models, and storing the results.

A set of reusable training helpers can simplify switching between regression and machine-learning models.

Example code
Model training 
target_outcomes <- c(
  exposure_a = "exposure_a_residual",
  exposure_b = "exposure_b_residual",
  exposure_c = "exposure_c_residual",
  exposure_d = "exposure_d_residual"
)

training_feature_matrix <- as.matrix(signature_training_df[, feature_columns])

get_best_lambda <- function(outcome_name) {
  response_vector <- signature_training_df[[outcome_name]]
  set.seed(123)
  cv_fit <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(
    x = training_feature_matrix,
    y = response_vector,
    alpha = 0,
    nfolds = 10
  )
  cv_fit$lambda.min
}

best_lambdas <- sapply(target_outcomes, get_best_lambda)

final_signature_models <- purrr::imap(target_outcomes, function(outcome_name, signature_name) {
  glmnet::glmnet(
    x = training_feature_matrix,
    y = signature_training_df[[outcome_name]],
    alpha = 0,
    lambda = best_lambdas[[signature_name]]
  )
})

# Example helper for elastic-net style linear regression
train_linear_signature <- function(train_data) {
  recipe_obj <- recipes::recipe(target ~ ., data = train_data) %>%
    recipes::step_rm(id)

  model_spec <- parsnip::linear_reg(penalty = 0.5, mixture = 0.5) %>%
    parsnip::set_engine("glmnet")

  workflows::workflow() %>%
    workflows::add_recipe(recipe_obj) %>%
    workflows::add_model(model_spec) %>%
    parsnip::fit(data = train_data) %>%
    purrr::pluck("fit")
}

# Example helper for random forest training
train_random_forest_signature <- function(train_data, mode = "regression") {
  recipe_obj <- recipes::recipe(target ~ ., data = train_data) %>%
    recipes::step_rm(id)

  model_spec <- parsnip::rand_forest() %>%
    parsnip::set_engine("ranger", importance = "impurity") %>%
    parsnip::set_mode(mode)

  workflows::workflow() %>%
    workflows::add_recipe(recipe_obj) %>%
    workflows::add_model(model_spec) %>%
    parsnip::fit(data = train_data) %>%
    purrr::pluck("fit")
}

# Example helper for k-nearest-neighbor training
train_knn_signature <- function(train_data, mode = "regression") {
  neighbors_n <- min(length(unique(train_data$target)), 10)

  recipe_obj <- recipes::recipe(target ~ ., data = train_data) %>%
    recipes::step_rm(id)

  model_spec <- parsnip::nearest_neighbor(
    neighbors = neighbors_n,
    weight_func = "triangular"
  ) %>%
    parsnip::set_engine("kknn") %>%
    parsnip::set_mode(mode)

  workflows::workflow() %>%
    workflows::add_recipe(recipe_obj) %>%
    workflows::add_model(model_spec) %>%
    parsnip::fit(data = train_data) %>%
    purrr::pluck("fit")
}

Score construction

After the final models are fitted, the participant-level MMS scores can be generated for the testing subset or for the full dataset.

A score-calculation helper can turn model coefficients into a sample-level weighted score after filtering to non-zero metabolite coefficients.

Example code
Score construction 
testing_feature_matrix <- as.matrix(signature_testing_df[, feature_columns])

predicted_signature_scores <- purrr::imap(
  final_signature_models,
  ~ as.vector(predict(.x, newx = testing_feature_matrix, s = best_lambdas[[.y]]))
)

signature_testing_results <- dplyr::bind_cols(
  signature_testing_df,
  as.data.frame(predicted_signature_scores)
)

# Example coefficient-based score calculation helper
calculate_signature_score <- function(ids, df, coefficients) {
  coefficient_df <- data.frame(
    metabolite = rownames(coefficients),
    coefficient = as.numeric(coefficients)
  )

  coefficient_df <- coefficient_df %>%
    dplyr::filter(metabolite != "(Intercept)") %>%
    dplyr::filter(metabolite != "id") %>%
    dplyr::filter(coefficient != 0)

  selected_features <- coefficient_df$metabolite

  weighted_score_matrix <- data.frame(
    matrix(NA, nrow = nrow(df), ncol = length(selected_features))
  )

  for (i in seq_along(selected_features)) {
    feature_name <- selected_features[i]
    feature_position <- which(coefficient_df$metabolite == feature_name)

    weighted_score_matrix[, i] <- df %>%
      dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(feature_name)) %>%
      dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(dplyr::everything(), ~ .x * coefficient_df[feature_position, "coefficient"])) %>%
      dplyr::pull(1)
  }

  colnames(weighted_score_matrix) <- selected_features

  dplyr::bind_cols(ids, weighted_score_matrix) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(total_score = rowSums(dplyr::across(dplyr::all_of(selected_features))))
}

LASSO metabolite selection

If the goal is sparse feature selection rather than retaining all metabolite predictors, the same workflow can be used with alpha = 1.

Example code
LASSO metabolite selection 
lasso_fit <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(
  x = training_feature_matrix,
  y = signature_training_df[["exposure_a_residual"]],
  alpha = 1,
  nfolds = 10
)

Elastic net models

Elastic net is useful when correlated metabolites should be partially retained while still allowing shrinkage and some variable selection.

Example code
Elastic net models 
elastic_net_fit <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(
  x = training_feature_matrix,
  y = signature_training_df[["exposure_a_residual"]],
  alpha = 0.5,
  nfolds = 10
)

Weighted metabolite scores

The predicted values from the penalized model become the MMS score. These can then be standardized for interpretation per standard deviation.

Example code
Weighted metabolite scores 
full_feature_matrix <- as.matrix(analysis_df[, feature_columns])

all_signature_scores <- purrr::imap(
  final_signature_models,
  ~ as.vector(predict(.x, newx = full_feature_matrix, s = best_lambdas[[.y]]))
)

analysis_df <- dplyr::bind_cols(
  analysis_df,
  purrr::set_names(as.data.frame(all_signature_scores), paste0("signature_score_", names(all_signature_scores)))
) %>%
  mutate(across(starts_with("signature_score_"), scale, .names = "{.col}_sd"))

Model evaluation

The original code evaluated whether the predicted MMS score reproduced the residualized target exposure in the held-out population.

R2

One simple validation step is to regress the standardized residualized exposure on the standardized MMS score and extract R^2.

Reusable testing helpers can return predictions and core regression metrics from fitted models.

Example code
R2 
evaluation_model_specs <- list(
  exposure_a = list(y = "exposure_a_residual_sd", x = "signature_score_exposure_a_sd"),
  exposure_b = list(y = "exposure_b_residual_sd", x = "signature_score_exposure_b_sd")
)

evaluation_models <- purrr::map(evaluation_model_specs, function(spec) {
  lm(
    formula = as.formula(paste(spec$y, "~", spec$x)),
    data = signature_testing_results
  )
})

evaluation_results <- purrr::imap_dfr(evaluation_models, function(fit, signature_name) {
  slope <- broom::tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
    dplyr::filter(term != "(Intercept)")
  fit_summary <- broom::glance(fit)

  slope %>%
    mutate(
      signature = signature_name,
      r_squared = fit_summary$r.squared,
      adj_r_sq = fit_summary$adj.r.squared
    )
})

RMSE

RMSE can be reported alongside R^2 if prediction error is of interest.

These metrics can also be returned directly by reusable testing helpers, which is convenient when comparing several model families.

Example code
RMSE 
rmse_value <- yardstick::rmse_vec(
  truth = signature_testing_results$exposure_a_residual_sd,
  estimate = signature_testing_results$signature_score_exposure_a_sd
)

# Example regression testing helpers
test_linear_signature <- function(model, test_data) {
  model_fit <- model$fit

  predictions <- stats::predict(model_fit, new_data = test_data) %>%
    dplyr::as_tibble() %>%
    dplyr::rename(predicted = .pred) %>%
    dplyr::bind_cols(test_data)

  model_coefficients <- broom::tidy(model_fit)
  rmse_value <- caret::RMSE(predictions$predicted, predictions$target)

  list(
    prediction = predictions,
    model_coefficients = model_coefficients,
    rmse_value = rmse_value
  )
}

test_random_forest_regression <- function(model, test_data) {
  model_fit <- model$fit

  predictions <- stats::predict(model_fit, new_data = test_data) %>%
    dplyr::as_tibble() %>%
    dplyr::rename(predicted = .pred) %>%
    dplyr::bind_cols(test_data)

  list(
    prediction = predictions,
    model_importance = vip::vi(model_fit),
    r_squared = caret::R2(predictions$predicted, predictions$target),
    rmse_value = caret::RMSE(predictions$predicted, predictions$target)
  )
}

test_knn_regression <- function(model, test_data) {
  model_fit <- model$fit

  predictions <- stats::predict(model_fit, new_data = test_data) %>%
    dplyr::as_tibble() %>%
    dplyr::rename(predicted = .pred) %>%
    dplyr::bind_cols(test_data)

  list(
    prediction = predictions,
    r_squared = caret::R2(predictions$predicted, predictions$target),
    rmse_value = caret::RMSE(predictions$predicted, predictions$target)
  )
}

test_cross_validated_model <- function(model, test_data, type = "regression") {
  predictions <- predict(model, newdata = test_data) %>%
    dplyr::as_tibble() %>%
    dplyr::rename(predicted = value) %>%
    dplyr::bind_cols(test_data)

  if (type == "regression") {
    list(
      df = predictions,
      RMSE = caret::RMSE(predictions$predicted, predictions$target)
    )
  } else {
    list(
      df = predictions,
      confusion_matrix = caret::confusionMatrix(predictions$predicted, test_data$target)
    )
  }
}

ROC / AUC

If the signature is evaluated against a binary downstream outcome, ROC curves and AUC can be added.

For classification settings, testing helpers can provide confusion-matrix-based evaluation.

Example code
ROC / AUC 
roc_auc_result <- yardstick::roc_auc(
  data = downstream_analysis_df,
  truth = outcome_case_status,
  .pred = signature_score_exposure_a_sd
)

# Example classification testing helpers
test_random_forest_classification <- function(model, test_data) {
  model_fit <- model$fit

  predictions <- stats::predict(model_fit, new_data = test_data) %>%
    dplyr::as_tibble() %>%
    dplyr::rename(predicted = .pred_class) %>%
    dplyr::bind_cols(test_data)

  list(
    prediction = predictions,
    model_importance = vip::vi(model_fit),
    confusion_matrix = caret::confusionMatrix(predictions$predicted, test_data$target)
  )
}

test_knn_classification <- function(model, test_data) {
  model_fit <- model$fit

  predictions <- dplyr::bind_cols(
    stats::predict(model_fit, new_data = test_data),
    stats::predict(model_fit, new_data = test_data, type = "prob")
  ) %>%
    dplyr::as_tibble() %>%
    dplyr::rename(predicted = .pred_class) %>%
    dplyr::bind_cols(test_data)

  list(
    prediction = predictions,
    confusion_matrix = caret::confusionMatrix(predictions$predicted, test_data$target)
  )
}

Calibration

Calibration is useful when the MMS is interpreted as a predictive score rather than only as an exposure biomarker.

Example code
Calibration 
# Placeholder for calibration workflow
# calibration_df <- compute_calibration(data = downstream_analysis_df, score = "signature_score_exposure_a_sd")

Also include:

Permutation testing

Permutation tests can be used when assessing whether predictive performance exceeds what would be expected by chance.

Example code
Permutation testing 
# Placeholder for permutation-based validation
# permuted_results <- run_permutation_test(training_feature_matrix, signature_training_df$exposure_a_residual)

Cross-validation results

Cross-validation outputs can be summarized by storing fold-specific coefficients and then computing medians and interquartile ranges.

If you prefer a more abstract interface, the cross-validation helpers above can be used as a lightweight wrapper pair for repeated cross-validated training and testing.

Example code
Cross-validation results 
coefficient_summary_list <- list()

for (signature_name in names(target_outcomes)) {
  outcome_name <- target_outcomes[[signature_name]]
  lambda_value <- best_lambdas[[signature_name]]
  fold_coefficients <- vector("list", length = length(cv_folds$splits))

  for (i in seq_along(cv_folds$splits)) {
    fold_training_df <- rsample::analysis(cv_folds$splits[[i]])
    x_fold <- as.matrix(fold_training_df[, feature_columns])
    y_fold <- fold_training_df[[outcome_name]]

    fold_fit <- glmnet::glmnet(x = x_fold, y = y_fold, alpha = 0, lambda = lambda_value)
    fold_coefficients[[i]] <- as.matrix(coef(fold_fit))
  }

  coefficient_df <- do.call(cbind, fold_coefficients)
  coefficient_df <- coefficient_df[rownames(coefficient_df) != "(Intercept)", , drop = FALSE]

  coefficient_summary_list[[signature_name]] <- as.data.frame(coefficient_df) %>%
    mutate(
      median = apply(.[, seq_len(ncol(.))], 1, quantile, probs = 0.5),
      q1 = apply(.[, seq_len(ncol(.))], 1, quantile, probs = 0.25),
      q3 = apply(.[, seq_len(ncol(.))], 1, quantile, probs = 0.75),
      feature_id = rownames(.)
    ) %>%
    left_join(feature_annotation_df, by = "feature_id")
}

Robustness and reproducibility

Once the MMS scores are created, they can be carried into downstream epidemiological models and sensitivity analyses.

Sensitivity analyses

The original project tested whether MMS-outcome associations were robust to alternative adjustment sets and interaction terms. A generic version is shown below for conditional logistic regression, but the same principle applies to other models.

Example code
Sensitivity analyses 
downstream_model_formulas <- list(
  "Base-adjusted" = ~ covariate_1 + covariate_2 + strata(matching_set_id),
  "Fully-adjusted" = ~ covariate_1 + covariate_2 + covariate_3 + covariate_4 + strata(matching_set_id),
  "Sensitivity-adjusted" = ~ covariate_1 + covariate_2 + covariate_3 + covariate_4 + sensitivity_covariate + strata(matching_set_id)
)

fit_conditional_model <- function(data, signature_name, model_label, rhs_formula) {
  predictor_name <- paste0("signature_score_", signature_name, "_sd")
  rhs_string <- as.character(rhs_formula)[2]
  full_formula <- as.formula(paste0("binary_outcome ~ ", predictor_name, " + ", rhs_string))

  survival::clogit(full_formula, data = data, method = "efron", robust = TRUE) %>%
    broom::tidy(conf.int = TRUE) %>%
    dplyr::filter(term == predictor_name)
}

This is the point in the workflow where the MMS score leaves the pure prediction setting and becomes an epidemiological exposure variable for downstream association models.

Stability of metabolite selection

Coefficient stability across cross-validation folds helps identify which metabolites consistently contribute to the signature.

Example code
Stability of metabolite selection 
top_features_table <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  lapply(names(coefficient_summary_list), function(signature_name) {
    coefficient_summary_list[[signature_name]] %>%
      mutate(signature = signature_name)
  })
) %>%
  group_by(signature) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(median)), .by_group = TRUE) %>%
  slice_head(n = 30)

Reproducibility across folds

For transparent reporting, it is useful to export coefficient summaries, downstream association tables, and figures derived from the same standardized workflow.

Example code
Reproducibility across folds 
readr::write_csv(top_features_table, "results/signature_top_features.csv")
readr::write_csv(evaluation_results, "results/signature_evaluation_results.csv")

This generic structure can be adapted to:

  • dietary exposures
  • clinical biomarkers
  • disease risk signatures
  • survival outcomes
  • interaction and subgroup analyses

The key idea is to keep the workflow modular: preprocess, split data, fit the MMS, validate the score, and then carry the standardized score into downstream epidemiological models.

Some reusable helper patterns that fit naturally into this workflow are:

  • simulation helpers for teaching and examples
  • preprocessing wrappers
  • train/test splitting helpers
  • single-metabolite screening helpers
  • model-training helpers for elastic net, random forest, and k-nearest neighbors
  • score-calculation helpers
  • regression and classification testing helpers
Source Code
---
title: "Analytical workflow"
---

This page presents a comprehensive and modular workflow for constructing a multimetabolite signature (MMS), covering all key steps from the raw data preparation to the downstream epidemiological applications. The framework is designed to be adaptable across studies.

I hope this offers a useful and inspiring starting point for developing your own MMS workflow! 🧪📊✨

# Data import and preprocessing {#data-import}

## Data structure {#data-structure}

For a supervised MMS approach, the minimum required variables are:

![](/images/data%20structure%20example.png){fig-align="center" fig-cap="Data structure example" width="57%"}

-   **subject identifiers**,

-   **metabolomics features**,

-   at least one **exposure variable**.

Additional elements can be included depending on the study design, such as:

-   multiple exposures (for parallel signature construction),

-   covariates for adjustment or residualization,

-   metadata linking feature IDs to metabolite annotations.

## Metabolomics platform {#metabolomics-platform}

The metabolomics matrix should be clearly separated from participant-level metadata. In practice, this means defining which columns correspond to metabolite predictors and which columns correspond to exposures, covariates, and outcomes.

If you want a synthetic example for method development or teaching, a small simulation helper can be useful.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Metabolomics platform"
# Example: keep a vector with the metabolite feature columns
feature_columns <- colnames(analysis_df)[feature_start_col:ncol(analysis_df)]

# Convert to model matrix
feature_matrix <- as.matrix(analysis_df[, feature_columns])

# Optional: synthetic example data
# analysis_df <- simulate_example_data()
```
:::

## Exposure variables {#exposure-variables}

The target exposure can be a single variable or several related variables if multiple signatures are built in parallel.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Exposure variables"
# Example target exposures for parallel MMS construction
target_outcomes <- c(
  exposure_a = "exposure_a_residual",
  exposure_b = "exposure_b_residual",
  exposure_c = "exposure_c_residual",
  exposure_d = "exposure_d_residual"
)
```
:::

## Outcome definitions {#outcome-definitions}

If the MMS will later be related to disease, mortality, or another endpoint, it is helpful to define those downstream datasets early so the training and testing workflow is explicit.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Outcome definitions"
evaluation_ids <- evaluation_registry_df %>%
  dplyr::pull(participant_id)
```
:::

Before fitting MMS models, the data typically undergo residualization, cleaning, and standardization steps.

Typical metabolomics steps:

## Missing value handling {#missing-value-handling}

Missing metabolite values should be addressed before model fitting, using a strategy aligned with the platform and study design.

A compact preprocessing helper can wrap missingness filtering, imputation, log transformation, and normalization in one step.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Missing value handling"
# Placeholder example
analysis_df <- analysis_df %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(feature_columns), ~ ifelse(is.na(.x), median(.x, na.rm = TRUE), .x)))

# Example reusable preprocessing helper
preprocess_metabolomics_data <- function(raw_data, id_col, target_col, feature_start_col,
                                         cutoff_columns = 0.2, cutoff_rows = 0.2,
                                         imputation = "median") {
  clean_data <- raw_data %>%
    tidyr::drop_na({{ target_col }}) %>%
    dplyr::distinct() %>%
    janitor::remove_empty("cols", cutoff = cutoff_columns) %>%
    janitor::remove_empty("rows", cutoff = cutoff_rows)

  variables <- clean_data[, seq_len(feature_start_col - 1)]
  metabolite_data <- clean_data[, feature_start_col:ncol(clean_data)]

  selected_data <- dplyr::bind_cols(
    dplyr::select(variables, {{ id_col }}, {{ target_col }}),
    metabolite_data
  )

  extra_covariates <- variables %>%
    dplyr::select(-{{ id_col }}, -{{ target_col }})

  preprocess_recipe <- recipes::recipe(~ ., data = selected_data) %>%
    recipes::update_role({{ id_col }}, new_role = "id") %>%
    recipes::update_role({{ target_col }}, new_role = "outcome")

  preprocess_recipe <- if (imputation == "knn") {
    preprocess_recipe %>% recipes::step_impute_knn(recipes::all_predictors())
  } else if (imputation == "lower") {
    preprocess_recipe %>% recipes::step_impute_lower(recipes::all_predictors())
  } else if (imputation == "mean") {
    preprocess_recipe %>% recipes::step_impute_mean(recipes::all_predictors())
  } else {
    preprocess_recipe %>% recipes::step_impute_median(recipes::all_predictors())
  }

  processed_data <- preprocess_recipe %>%
    recipes::step_log(recipes::all_predictors()) %>%
    recipes::step_normalize(recipes::all_predictors()) %>%
    recipes::prep(training = selected_data) %>%
    recipes::juice() %>%
    dplyr::rename(id = {{ id_col }}, target = {{ target_col }})

  dplyr::bind_cols(extra_covariates, processed_data)
}
```
:::

## Normalization {#normalization}

Normalization may be applied at the metabolite-feature level depending on the platform and preprocessing pipeline used upstream.

In the helper function above, normalization is performed through a `recipes` workflow after imputation and log transformation.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Normalization"
# Placeholder example for already selected feature columns
analysis_df <- analysis_df %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(feature_columns), ~ .x / sd(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))
```
:::

## Transformation (log scaling) {#transformation-log-scaling}

Log transformation is often used to reduce skewness and stabilize variance.

This is also built into the helper function above.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Transformation (log scaling)"
analysis_df <- analysis_df %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(feature_columns), log1p))
```
:::

## Batch correction {#batch-correction}

If samples were processed in multiple analytical batches, batch correction should occur before signature construction.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Batch correction"
# Placeholder: use the batch variable defined in your project
# analysis_df <- correct_batch_effects(analysis_df, batch_var = "batch_id", features = feature_columns)
```
:::

## Scaling (z-score) {#scaling-z-score}

Standardizing predictors is especially important for penalized regression models.

Again, the preprocessing helper already standardizes predictors after transformation, which is a useful template if you want a single entry point for data wrangling.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Scaling (z-score)"
analysis_df <- analysis_df %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(feature_columns), scale))
```
:::

# Study design & sample splitting {#study-design-sample-splitting}

This section shows how to define training and testing populations in a reusable way.

## Training vs test dataset {#training-vs-test-dataset}

At minimum, an MMS workflow requires both a **training dataset** and a **testing dataset**. These can be obtained in two ways:

-   by using two separate datasets (one for model training and the other for external validation), or by defining subsets of individuals within a single dataset that are reserved for testing. In both cases, the datasets or subsets must contain the same variables and be similarly structured, or

-   by splitting a single dataset into training and testing subsets (e.g., random split).

![](/images/train_test.png){fig-align="center" fig-cap="Training and testing dataset strategies" width="57%"}

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code
```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "User-defined training/test individuals"
#| 
  # Participants reserved for external or downstream evaluation
evaluation_ids <- external_registry_df %>%
  dplyr::pull(participant_id)

# Training population
train_data <- data %>%
  filter(!participant_id %in% evaluation_ids)

# Testing or external evaluation population
test_data <- data %>%
  filter(participant_id %in% evaluation_ids)

```

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Random training/test splitting helper"

# Example helper-based split
split_dataset <- function(data, training_prop = 0.7, strata = NULL) {
  split_obj <- if (is.null(strata)) {
    rsample::initial_split(data, prop = training_prop)
  } else {
    rsample::initial_split(data, prop = training_prop, strata = {{ strata }})
  }

  list(
    split = split_obj,
    train_data = rsample::training(split_obj),
    test_data = rsample::testing(split_obj)
  )
}
```
:::

::: {.callout-note .callout-strata-note collapse="true"}
## `strata`

This note refers to the optional `strata` argument inside `split_dataset()`.

Stratification helps keep the training and testing sets similar with respect to an important variable, usually the outcome or exposure grouping.This makes model training and evaluation more stable and more comparable. For example:

-   If your outcome is binary and rare, a purely random split might put too few cases in the test set.
-   If your target has strong class imbalance, stratification preserves roughly the same proportion in both subsets.

Use stratification when:
-   you have a categorical outcome like case/control
-   you have strong imbalance between groups
-   you want both train and test to represent the same population structure

You usually do not need it when:
-   the dataset is large and well balanced
-   the outcome is continuous and you are doing a simple random split
:::

::: {.callout-note .callout-strata-note collapse="true"}
## `training_prop = 0.7`

This note refers to the optional `training_prop` argument inside `split_dataset()`.

This argument defines the proportion of individuals kept in the training dataset.

If you do not specify it, the default is `0.7`, meaning that 70% of individuals are assigned to the training dataset and the remaining 30% to the testing dataset.
:::

## Cross-validation {#cross-validation}

Cross-validation is used to tune the regularization parameter and estimate the stability of the model.

If you want a more packaged training interface, a helper can wrap repeated cross-validation with `caret::train()`.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Cross-validation"
set.seed(123)
cv_folds <- rsample::vfold_cv(signature_training_df, v = 10)

# Example helper for repeated cross-validation
cross_validated_model <- function(train_data, model = "glmnet", nfolds = 10,
                                  nrepeats = 5, tune_length = 10) {
  control <- caret::trainControl(
    method = "repeatedcv",
    number = nfolds,
    repeats = nrepeats,
    search = "random",
    verboseIter = TRUE
  )

  set.seed(42)
  caret::train(
    target ~ .,
    data = train_data,
    method = model,
    preProcess = c("center", "scale"),
    tuneLength = tune_length,
    trControl = control
  )
}
```
:::

## Internal validation {#internal-validation}

Internal validation can be performed by predicting the held-out fold or a pre-specified testing subset and then quantifying how well the MMS reproduces the target exposure.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Internal validation"
# Predictor matrix for training
training_feature_matrix <- as.matrix(signature_training_df[, feature_columns])

# Predictor matrix for testing
testing_feature_matrix <- as.matrix(signature_testing_df[, feature_columns])
```
:::

## Avoiding overfitting {#avoiding-overfitting}

Key strategies include:

-   separating tuning from final model fitting
-   evaluating performance in a held-out dataset
-   reporting coefficient stability across folds
-   using sensitivity analyses for downstream associations

# Building the MMS {#building-the-mms}

This section contains a generic template based on the original analysis: residualize the target exposure, tune a penalized model, fit the signature, and generate participant-level scores.

## Feature selection {#feature-selection}

If the exposure of interest should first be adjusted for major confounders or correlated dietary components, residualization can be used before signature training.

For a single-metabolite screening step before multivariable signature building, a helper can fit metabolite-wise models, apply multiple testing correction, and return both the filtered results and residuals.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Feature selection"
# Example: residualize multiple target exposures against shared covariates
residual_model_specs <- list(
  exposure_a = exposure_a ~ total_energy + covariate_1 + covariate_2 + covariate_3,
  exposure_b = exposure_b ~ total_energy + covariate_1 + covariate_2 + covariate_3 + exposure_a,
  exposure_c = exposure_c ~ total_energy + covariate_1 + covariate_2 + covariate_3 + exposure_a + exposure_b,
  exposure_d = exposure_d ~ total_energy + covariate_1 + covariate_2 + covariate_3 + exposure_a + exposure_b
)

residual_models <- purrr::map(
  residual_model_specs,
  ~ lm(.x, data = analysis_df)
)

analysis_df <- analysis_df %>%
  mutate(
    exposure_a_residual = residuals(residual_models$exposure_a),
    exposure_b_residual = residuals(residual_models$exposure_b),
    exposure_c_residual = residuals(residual_models$exposure_c),
    exposure_d_residual = residuals(residual_models$exposure_d)
  )

# Example single-metabolite screening helper
single_metabolite_analysis <- function(data, train_data, exposure_feature,
                                       feature_start_col, covariates = NULL,
                                       threshold = 0.01, correction = NULL) {
  metabolite_columns <- colnames(data)[feature_start_col:ncol(data)]
  features <- c(exposure_feature, covariates)

  fit_single_model <- function(metabolite_name, predictors, dataset) {
    model_formula <- stats::reformulate(predictors, response = metabolite_name)
    stats::lm(model_formula, data = dataset) %>%
      broom::tidy() %>%
      dplyr::mutate(yvar = metabolite_name, .before = dplyr::everything())
  }

  extract_residuals <- function(metabolite_name, predictors, dataset) {
    model_formula <- stats::reformulate(predictors, response = metabolite_name)
    stats::lm(model_formula, data = dataset) %>%
      residuals() %>%
      tibble::tibble(value = .)
  }

  results <- metabolite_columns %>%
    furrr::future_map(~ fit_single_model(.x, features, dataset = train_data)) %>%
    purrr::list_rbind(names_to = "model_id")

  residuals_df <- metabolite_columns %>%
    furrr::future_map(~ extract_residuals(.x, features, dataset = data)) %>%
    purrr::list_cbind()

  colnames(residuals_df) <- metabolite_columns

  filtered_results <- if (length(correction)) {
    results %>%
      dplyr::mutate(p_value_corrected = stats::p.adjust(p.value, method = correction)) %>%
      dplyr::filter(stringr::str_detect(term, "target")) %>%
      dplyr::filter(p_value_corrected < threshold) %>%
      dplyr::arrange(p_value_corrected)
  } else {
    results %>%
      dplyr::filter(stringr::str_detect(term, "target")) %>%
      dplyr::filter(p.value < threshold) %>%
      dplyr::arrange(p.value)
  }

  list(
    results = filtered_results,
    residuals = residuals_df %>% dplyr::select(filtered_results$yvar)
  )
}
```
:::

## Model training {#model-training}

The original project used ridge regression (`alpha = 0`) to derive signatures for multiple related exposures. Below is a generic template for tuning lambda, fitting the models, and storing the results.

A set of reusable training helpers can simplify switching between regression and machine-learning models.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Model training"
target_outcomes <- c(
  exposure_a = "exposure_a_residual",
  exposure_b = "exposure_b_residual",
  exposure_c = "exposure_c_residual",
  exposure_d = "exposure_d_residual"
)

training_feature_matrix <- as.matrix(signature_training_df[, feature_columns])

get_best_lambda <- function(outcome_name) {
  response_vector <- signature_training_df[[outcome_name]]
  set.seed(123)
  cv_fit <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(
    x = training_feature_matrix,
    y = response_vector,
    alpha = 0,
    nfolds = 10
  )
  cv_fit$lambda.min
}

best_lambdas <- sapply(target_outcomes, get_best_lambda)

final_signature_models <- purrr::imap(target_outcomes, function(outcome_name, signature_name) {
  glmnet::glmnet(
    x = training_feature_matrix,
    y = signature_training_df[[outcome_name]],
    alpha = 0,
    lambda = best_lambdas[[signature_name]]
  )
})

# Example helper for elastic-net style linear regression
train_linear_signature <- function(train_data) {
  recipe_obj <- recipes::recipe(target ~ ., data = train_data) %>%
    recipes::step_rm(id)

  model_spec <- parsnip::linear_reg(penalty = 0.5, mixture = 0.5) %>%
    parsnip::set_engine("glmnet")

  workflows::workflow() %>%
    workflows::add_recipe(recipe_obj) %>%
    workflows::add_model(model_spec) %>%
    parsnip::fit(data = train_data) %>%
    purrr::pluck("fit")
}

# Example helper for random forest training
train_random_forest_signature <- function(train_data, mode = "regression") {
  recipe_obj <- recipes::recipe(target ~ ., data = train_data) %>%
    recipes::step_rm(id)

  model_spec <- parsnip::rand_forest() %>%
    parsnip::set_engine("ranger", importance = "impurity") %>%
    parsnip::set_mode(mode)

  workflows::workflow() %>%
    workflows::add_recipe(recipe_obj) %>%
    workflows::add_model(model_spec) %>%
    parsnip::fit(data = train_data) %>%
    purrr::pluck("fit")
}

# Example helper for k-nearest-neighbor training
train_knn_signature <- function(train_data, mode = "regression") {
  neighbors_n <- min(length(unique(train_data$target)), 10)

  recipe_obj <- recipes::recipe(target ~ ., data = train_data) %>%
    recipes::step_rm(id)

  model_spec <- parsnip::nearest_neighbor(
    neighbors = neighbors_n,
    weight_func = "triangular"
  ) %>%
    parsnip::set_engine("kknn") %>%
    parsnip::set_mode(mode)

  workflows::workflow() %>%
    workflows::add_recipe(recipe_obj) %>%
    workflows::add_model(model_spec) %>%
    parsnip::fit(data = train_data) %>%
    purrr::pluck("fit")
}
```
:::

## Score construction {#score-construction}

After the final models are fitted, the participant-level MMS scores can be generated for the testing subset or for the full dataset.

A score-calculation helper can turn model coefficients into a sample-level weighted score after filtering to non-zero metabolite coefficients.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Score construction"
testing_feature_matrix <- as.matrix(signature_testing_df[, feature_columns])

predicted_signature_scores <- purrr::imap(
  final_signature_models,
  ~ as.vector(predict(.x, newx = testing_feature_matrix, s = best_lambdas[[.y]]))
)

signature_testing_results <- dplyr::bind_cols(
  signature_testing_df,
  as.data.frame(predicted_signature_scores)
)

# Example coefficient-based score calculation helper
calculate_signature_score <- function(ids, df, coefficients) {
  coefficient_df <- data.frame(
    metabolite = rownames(coefficients),
    coefficient = as.numeric(coefficients)
  )

  coefficient_df <- coefficient_df %>%
    dplyr::filter(metabolite != "(Intercept)") %>%
    dplyr::filter(metabolite != "id") %>%
    dplyr::filter(coefficient != 0)

  selected_features <- coefficient_df$metabolite

  weighted_score_matrix <- data.frame(
    matrix(NA, nrow = nrow(df), ncol = length(selected_features))
  )

  for (i in seq_along(selected_features)) {
    feature_name <- selected_features[i]
    feature_position <- which(coefficient_df$metabolite == feature_name)

    weighted_score_matrix[, i] <- df %>%
      dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(feature_name)) %>%
      dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(dplyr::everything(), ~ .x * coefficient_df[feature_position, "coefficient"])) %>%
      dplyr::pull(1)
  }

  colnames(weighted_score_matrix) <- selected_features

  dplyr::bind_cols(ids, weighted_score_matrix) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(total_score = rowSums(dplyr::across(dplyr::all_of(selected_features))))
}
```
:::

## LASSO metabolite selection {#lasso-metabolite-selection}

If the goal is sparse feature selection rather than retaining all metabolite predictors, the same workflow can be used with `alpha = 1`.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "LASSO metabolite selection"
lasso_fit <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(
  x = training_feature_matrix,
  y = signature_training_df[["exposure_a_residual"]],
  alpha = 1,
  nfolds = 10
)
```
:::

## Elastic net models {#elastic-net-models}

Elastic net is useful when correlated metabolites should be partially retained while still allowing shrinkage and some variable selection.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Elastic net models"
elastic_net_fit <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(
  x = training_feature_matrix,
  y = signature_training_df[["exposure_a_residual"]],
  alpha = 0.5,
  nfolds = 10
)
```
:::

## Weighted metabolite scores {#weighted-metabolite-scores}

The predicted values from the penalized model become the MMS score. These can then be standardized for interpretation per standard deviation.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Weighted metabolite scores"
full_feature_matrix <- as.matrix(analysis_df[, feature_columns])

all_signature_scores <- purrr::imap(
  final_signature_models,
  ~ as.vector(predict(.x, newx = full_feature_matrix, s = best_lambdas[[.y]]))
)

analysis_df <- dplyr::bind_cols(
  analysis_df,
  purrr::set_names(as.data.frame(all_signature_scores), paste0("signature_score_", names(all_signature_scores)))
) %>%
  mutate(across(starts_with("signature_score_"), scale, .names = "{.col}_sd"))
```
:::

# Model evaluation {#model-evaluation}

The original code evaluated whether the predicted MMS score reproduced the residualized target exposure in the held-out population.

## R2 {#r2}

One simple validation step is to regress the standardized residualized exposure on the standardized MMS score and extract `R^2`.

Reusable testing helpers can return predictions and core regression metrics from fitted models.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "R2"
evaluation_model_specs <- list(
  exposure_a = list(y = "exposure_a_residual_sd", x = "signature_score_exposure_a_sd"),
  exposure_b = list(y = "exposure_b_residual_sd", x = "signature_score_exposure_b_sd")
)

evaluation_models <- purrr::map(evaluation_model_specs, function(spec) {
  lm(
    formula = as.formula(paste(spec$y, "~", spec$x)),
    data = signature_testing_results
  )
})

evaluation_results <- purrr::imap_dfr(evaluation_models, function(fit, signature_name) {
  slope <- broom::tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
    dplyr::filter(term != "(Intercept)")
  fit_summary <- broom::glance(fit)

  slope %>%
    mutate(
      signature = signature_name,
      r_squared = fit_summary$r.squared,
      adj_r_sq = fit_summary$adj.r.squared
    )
})
```
:::

## RMSE {#rmse}

RMSE can be reported alongside `R^2` if prediction error is of interest.

These metrics can also be returned directly by reusable testing helpers, which is convenient when comparing several model families.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "RMSE"
rmse_value <- yardstick::rmse_vec(
  truth = signature_testing_results$exposure_a_residual_sd,
  estimate = signature_testing_results$signature_score_exposure_a_sd
)

# Example regression testing helpers
test_linear_signature <- function(model, test_data) {
  model_fit <- model$fit

  predictions <- stats::predict(model_fit, new_data = test_data) %>%
    dplyr::as_tibble() %>%
    dplyr::rename(predicted = .pred) %>%
    dplyr::bind_cols(test_data)

  model_coefficients <- broom::tidy(model_fit)
  rmse_value <- caret::RMSE(predictions$predicted, predictions$target)

  list(
    prediction = predictions,
    model_coefficients = model_coefficients,
    rmse_value = rmse_value
  )
}

test_random_forest_regression <- function(model, test_data) {
  model_fit <- model$fit

  predictions <- stats::predict(model_fit, new_data = test_data) %>%
    dplyr::as_tibble() %>%
    dplyr::rename(predicted = .pred) %>%
    dplyr::bind_cols(test_data)

  list(
    prediction = predictions,
    model_importance = vip::vi(model_fit),
    r_squared = caret::R2(predictions$predicted, predictions$target),
    rmse_value = caret::RMSE(predictions$predicted, predictions$target)
  )
}

test_knn_regression <- function(model, test_data) {
  model_fit <- model$fit

  predictions <- stats::predict(model_fit, new_data = test_data) %>%
    dplyr::as_tibble() %>%
    dplyr::rename(predicted = .pred) %>%
    dplyr::bind_cols(test_data)

  list(
    prediction = predictions,
    r_squared = caret::R2(predictions$predicted, predictions$target),
    rmse_value = caret::RMSE(predictions$predicted, predictions$target)
  )
}

test_cross_validated_model <- function(model, test_data, type = "regression") {
  predictions <- predict(model, newdata = test_data) %>%
    dplyr::as_tibble() %>%
    dplyr::rename(predicted = value) %>%
    dplyr::bind_cols(test_data)

  if (type == "regression") {
    list(
      df = predictions,
      RMSE = caret::RMSE(predictions$predicted, predictions$target)
    )
  } else {
    list(
      df = predictions,
      confusion_matrix = caret::confusionMatrix(predictions$predicted, test_data$target)
    )
  }
}
```
:::

## ROC / AUC {#roc-auc}

If the signature is evaluated against a binary downstream outcome, ROC curves and AUC can be added.

For classification settings, testing helpers can provide confusion-matrix-based evaluation.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "ROC / AUC"
roc_auc_result <- yardstick::roc_auc(
  data = downstream_analysis_df,
  truth = outcome_case_status,
  .pred = signature_score_exposure_a_sd
)

# Example classification testing helpers
test_random_forest_classification <- function(model, test_data) {
  model_fit <- model$fit

  predictions <- stats::predict(model_fit, new_data = test_data) %>%
    dplyr::as_tibble() %>%
    dplyr::rename(predicted = .pred_class) %>%
    dplyr::bind_cols(test_data)

  list(
    prediction = predictions,
    model_importance = vip::vi(model_fit),
    confusion_matrix = caret::confusionMatrix(predictions$predicted, test_data$target)
  )
}

test_knn_classification <- function(model, test_data) {
  model_fit <- model$fit

  predictions <- dplyr::bind_cols(
    stats::predict(model_fit, new_data = test_data),
    stats::predict(model_fit, new_data = test_data, type = "prob")
  ) %>%
    dplyr::as_tibble() %>%
    dplyr::rename(predicted = .pred_class) %>%
    dplyr::bind_cols(test_data)

  list(
    prediction = predictions,
    confusion_matrix = caret::confusionMatrix(predictions$predicted, test_data$target)
  )
}
```
:::

## Calibration {#calibration}

Calibration is useful when the MMS is interpreted as a predictive score rather than only as an exposure biomarker.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Calibration"
# Placeholder for calibration workflow
# calibration_df <- compute_calibration(data = downstream_analysis_df, score = "signature_score_exposure_a_sd")
```
:::

Also include:

## Permutation testing {#permutation-testing}

Permutation tests can be used when assessing whether predictive performance exceeds what would be expected by chance.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Permutation testing"
# Placeholder for permutation-based validation
# permuted_results <- run_permutation_test(training_feature_matrix, signature_training_df$exposure_a_residual)
```
:::

## Cross-validation results {#cross-validation-results}

Cross-validation outputs can be summarized by storing fold-specific coefficients and then computing medians and interquartile ranges.

If you prefer a more abstract interface, the cross-validation helpers above can be used as a lightweight wrapper pair for repeated cross-validated training and testing.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Cross-validation results"
coefficient_summary_list <- list()

for (signature_name in names(target_outcomes)) {
  outcome_name <- target_outcomes[[signature_name]]
  lambda_value <- best_lambdas[[signature_name]]
  fold_coefficients <- vector("list", length = length(cv_folds$splits))

  for (i in seq_along(cv_folds$splits)) {
    fold_training_df <- rsample::analysis(cv_folds$splits[[i]])
    x_fold <- as.matrix(fold_training_df[, feature_columns])
    y_fold <- fold_training_df[[outcome_name]]

    fold_fit <- glmnet::glmnet(x = x_fold, y = y_fold, alpha = 0, lambda = lambda_value)
    fold_coefficients[[i]] <- as.matrix(coef(fold_fit))
  }

  coefficient_df <- do.call(cbind, fold_coefficients)
  coefficient_df <- coefficient_df[rownames(coefficient_df) != "(Intercept)", , drop = FALSE]

  coefficient_summary_list[[signature_name]] <- as.data.frame(coefficient_df) %>%
    mutate(
      median = apply(.[, seq_len(ncol(.))], 1, quantile, probs = 0.5),
      q1 = apply(.[, seq_len(ncol(.))], 1, quantile, probs = 0.25),
      q3 = apply(.[, seq_len(ncol(.))], 1, quantile, probs = 0.75),
      feature_id = rownames(.)
    ) %>%
    left_join(feature_annotation_df, by = "feature_id")
}
```
:::

# Robustness and reproducibility {#robustness-and-reproducibility}

Once the MMS scores are created, they can be carried into downstream epidemiological models and sensitivity analyses.

## Sensitivity analyses {#sensitivity-analyses}

The original project tested whether MMS-outcome associations were robust to alternative adjustment sets and interaction terms. A generic version is shown below for conditional logistic regression, but the same principle applies to other models.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Sensitivity analyses"
downstream_model_formulas <- list(
  "Base-adjusted" = ~ covariate_1 + covariate_2 + strata(matching_set_id),
  "Fully-adjusted" = ~ covariate_1 + covariate_2 + covariate_3 + covariate_4 + strata(matching_set_id),
  "Sensitivity-adjusted" = ~ covariate_1 + covariate_2 + covariate_3 + covariate_4 + sensitivity_covariate + strata(matching_set_id)
)

fit_conditional_model <- function(data, signature_name, model_label, rhs_formula) {
  predictor_name <- paste0("signature_score_", signature_name, "_sd")
  rhs_string <- as.character(rhs_formula)[2]
  full_formula <- as.formula(paste0("binary_outcome ~ ", predictor_name, " + ", rhs_string))

  survival::clogit(full_formula, data = data, method = "efron", robust = TRUE) %>%
    broom::tidy(conf.int = TRUE) %>%
    dplyr::filter(term == predictor_name)
}
```
:::

This is the point in the workflow where the MMS score leaves the pure prediction setting and becomes an epidemiological exposure variable for downstream association models.

## Stability of metabolite selection {#stability-of-metabolite-selection}

Coefficient stability across cross-validation folds helps identify which metabolites consistently contribute to the signature.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Stability of metabolite selection"
top_features_table <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  lapply(names(coefficient_summary_list), function(signature_name) {
    coefficient_summary_list[[signature_name]] %>%
      mutate(signature = signature_name)
  })
) %>%
  group_by(signature) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(median)), .by_group = TRUE) %>%
  slice_head(n = 30)
```
:::

## Reproducibility across folds {#reproducibility-across-folds}

For transparent reporting, it is useful to export coefficient summaries, downstream association tables, and figures derived from the same standardized workflow.

::: {.callout-note .callout-code-example}
## Example code

```{r, eval=FALSE}
#| code-summary: "Reproducibility across folds"
readr::write_csv(top_features_table, "results/signature_top_features.csv")
readr::write_csv(evaluation_results, "results/signature_evaluation_results.csv")
```
:::

This generic structure can be adapted to:

-   dietary exposures
-   clinical biomarkers
-   disease risk signatures
-   survival outcomes
-   interaction and subgroup analyses

The key idea is to keep the workflow modular: preprocess, split data, fit the MMS, validate the score, and then carry the standardized score into downstream epidemiological models.

Some reusable helper patterns that fit naturally into this workflow are:

-   simulation helpers for teaching and examples
-   preprocessing wrappers
-   train/test splitting helpers
-   single-metabolite screening helpers
-   model-training helpers for elastic net, random forest, and k-nearest neighbors
-   score-calculation helpers
-   regression and classification testing helpers
